This is the standard shape of a Python project. The specific tools you choose can vary but the workflow is the same.

title: How It Connects
text:
Tools in the workflow
The five-step workflow (Organize → Write → Test → Share → Deploy).
Organize — Virtual environment + package manager
Before you write a single line, you create a virtual environment for your project and install the libraries it needs. This isolates your project from all others on your machine. The virtual environment and a requirements.txt file together make your project reproducible anywhere.
Write — IDE + AI assistant
Your IDE is where you spend most of your time. It understands your code, shows errors as you type, lets you navigate your project instantly, and runs your programs. Your AI assistant lives alongside it: either as an inline suggestion engine (Copilot), a chat panel in the sidebar, or both. The two work together: the IDE understands your project structure; the AI helps you build it.
Test — IDE debugger + AI for diagnosis
When something breaks, your IDE’s debugger lets you pause execution and inspect every variable. Your AI assistant reads error messages and tracebacks and explains what went wrong. Together, they turn debugging from a frustrating solo exercise into a guided diagnosis. AI can also help you write tests before a bug appears — the same loop, applied proactively.
Share — Git + GitHub
Git tracks every change you make, building a history you can always return to. GitHub hosts that history in the cloud, making it shareable and backed up. When you’re working with others (or just with your future self), Git branches let you experiment without breaking working code. Pull Requests on GitHub are how code gets reviewed before it merges.
Deploy — CI/CD + cloud hosting
Once your code is on GitHub, automated pipelines (GitHub Actions, etc.) can run your tests, build your application, and deploy it to a server every time you push. For most Python projects starting out, this step is optional — but it’s where the workflow ultimately leads.

title: How It Connects
text:
Where AI fits in
Before you write
Ask AI to help you plan: “How should I structure this project?” or “What’s the standard approach for this kind of data processing?” Getting the architecture right before writing saves hours of refactoring.
While you write
Inline suggestions (Copilot, JetBrains AI) complete what you’re typing. A comment describing intent is often enough to get the full function. Tab to accept; keep typing to ignore.
When you’re stuck
Paste your code and the error into the chat panel and ask what’s wrong. Faster than Stack Overflow. The AI can also explain any function, library, or language feature you don’t understand.
After it works
Ask AI to review your working code: “Is there a more Pythonic way to write this?” or “Are there edge cases I’m missing?” AI catches issues a human reviewer might skim past.
For testing
Ask AI to generate unit tests for your functions. It reads the function’s logic and proposes test cases, including edge cases you might not have thought of. You review and refine.
For documentation
Ask AI to write docstrings, README files, and inline comments. The mechanical parts of documentation are exactly what AI does well — you provide the judgment about what’s important.

title: How It Connects
text:
The standard project structure
Python projects follow a set of widely recognized conventions. When you open any well-structured project, you’ll find these same files and folders.
my-project/
├── .venv/          ← virtual environment (never committed to Git)
├── src/
│   ├── main.py     ← start the program, call the main function
│   ├── analysis.py ← main function/logic
│   └── utils.py    ← custom reusable functions for analysis
├── tests/
│   └── test_analysis.py
├── .gitignore      ← tells Git what NOT to track
├── requirements.txt ← list of packages needed
└── README.md       ← what this project is and how to run it

.venv/: Your virtual environment lives here. It’s specific to your machine and can always be recreated from requirements.txt, so it is never committed to Git. This is the most important thing to put in your .gitignore.
.gitignore: Tells Git which files to skip. At minimum: .venv/, __pycache__/, *.pyc, and .env (environment variables and secrets, never commit these).
requirements.txt: The list of packages your project needs. Anyone who clones your project runs pip install -r requirements.txt to recreate your environment exactly.
README.md: The first thing anyone reads when they open your project on GitHub. Write what the project does, how to set it up, and how to run it.


← The Tools | Get Started →

title: Get Started
text: The whole workflow. Do this first.

title: Get Started
text:
First steps

Prerequisites
Make sure you have:

Python: python.org
pip: included with Python; on some systems use pip3 and python3 instead of pip and python
Git: git-scm.com
GitHub CLI: cli.github.com
A GitHub account: github.com



Step 1: Organize — Folder, Git, virtual environment, and files

Windows
Press Windows + X then I to open PowerShell. Type these one at a time:
cd Desktop             # navigate to your Desktop
mkdir tip_calculator   # create your project folder
cd tip_calculator      # move into it
code .                 # open VS Code here


Mac
Open Terminal from Spotlight (Cmd + Space, type “Terminal”). Type these one at a time:
cd Desktop             # navigate to your Desktop
mkdir tip_calculator   # create your project folder
cd tip_calculator      # move into it
code .                 # open VS Code here

Switch to the terminal inside VS Code with Ctrl + ` and use this for everything from here on.
Tell Git who you are (one-time setup, never needs repeating):
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"   # your GitHub email
git config --global user.name "Your Name"           # your name
Then set up your project:
git init               # create a local git repository in this folder
python -m venv .venv   # create a virtual environment called .venv


Windows (activate venv)
.venv\Scripts\activate   # activate it — your prompt will show (.venv)


Mac (activate venv)
source .venv/bin/activate   # activate it — your prompt will show (.venv)
Create your project files:


Windows
New-Item tip_calculator.py        # your main code file
New-Item test_tip_calculator.py   # your test file
New-Item .gitignore               # tells Git what folders to skip


Mac
touch tip_calculator.py        # your main code file
touch test_tip_calculator.py   # your test file
touch .gitignore               # tells Git what folders to skip
Open .gitignore in VS Code, paste the following in, and save with Ctrl + S (Windows) or Cmd + S (Mac):
.venv/          # your virtual environment — hundreds of files, not needed on GitHub
__pycache__/    # Python's temp files, auto-created when you run code
.pytest_cache/  # pytest's temp files, auto-created when you run tests
None of these belong on GitHub. They are local, auto-generated, and irrelevant to anyone else.



Step 2: Install packages
Do this before writing any code so VS Code doesn’t show errors as you type:
pip install pytest             # install pytest into your .venv
pip freeze > requirements.txt  # save your package list
requirements.txt is how you tell anyone who clones your project which packages to install. It replaces the need to share your .venv folder.

If your virtual environment stops working (common on Python 3.13)
A known issue with Python 3.13 can break .venv after certain operations. If you see errors about missing modules or an inactive environment, delete it and start over:
Windows:
Remove-Item -Recurse -Force .venv
python -m venv .venv
.venv\Scripts\activate
pip install pytest
pip freeze > requirements.txt
Mac:
rm -rf .venv
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install pytest
pip freeze > requirements.txt



Step 3: Write — Add your code and tests
pytest is a package that checks whether your code does what you expect. You write a separate test file containing functions that call your real code with specific inputs and assert what the output should be. When you run pytest in the terminal, it finds those functions automatically and runs them all. A dot means a test passed. An F means it failed, and pytest tells you exactly what went wrong.
Open tip_calculator.py and paste this in, then save:
def calculate_tip(bill_amount, tip_percent):
    """Calculate the tip amount for a bill."""
    if bill_amount < 0:
        raise ValueError("Bill amount cannot be negative")
    return round(bill_amount * (tip_percent / 100), 2)
Open test_tip_calculator.py and paste this in, then save:
import pytest
from tip_calculator import calculate_tip

def test_standard_tip():
    assert calculate_tip(100, 20) == 20.0

def test_zero_tip():
    assert calculate_tip(50, 0) == 0.0

def test_negative_bill_raises_error():
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        calculate_tip(-10, 20)


Step 4: Test — Run pytest
pytest   # runs all test files it finds automatically
You should see:
collected 3 items
test_tip_calculator.py ...
3 passed in 0.12s
After this runs, two new folders will appear in VS Code’s file explorer — __pycache__/ and .pytest_cache/. pytest created these automatically. You don’t need to touch them — your .gitignore already tells Git to ignore them.


Step 5: Share — Push to GitHub
Log in to GitHub from the terminal (one-time setup):
gh auth login   # follow the prompts, it will open a browser to confirm
Then create your repo and push your code in one command:
gh repo create tip_calculator --private --source=. --remote=origin --push
# creates the repo, links it, and pushes your code
Go to github.com/yourusername/tip_calculator — your files are there.


The core loop going forward
edit code                                  # make changes in VS Code
pytest                                     # confirm everything still works
git add .                                  # stage all changes
git commit -m "describe what you changed"  # save a snapshot
git push                                   # send it to GitHub
Every git commit is a saved snapshot. Every git push sends it to GitHub.

title: Get Started
text:
What you just did
You completed the entire software development lifecycle: from an empty folder to a working project on GitHub with automated tests, version history, and a repeatable workflow.
Your .venv isolates dependencies. Your pytest suite ensures code correctness. Your Git history is a record you can revert to. Your GitHub repo is your backup and collaboration mechanism.
The loop you now run (edit → test → commit → push) is the same loop used to build production systems at scale.
Everything that comes next in Levels 2, 3, and 4 builds on this foundation. The next levels show you how to move faster within it.

title: Get Started
text:
What’s next

Level 2: AI inside your editor
Give the AI a goal. It reads your files, proposes changes across multiple files, and you review the diffs. Walkthrough in VS Code.
Go to Level 2


Level 3: Coding agent in your terminal
The AI runs in your terminal with access to your full environment. It reads files, executes code, installs packages, and iterates until the task is done.
Go to Level 3

title: Level 2: AI in Your Editor
text: Ignore 80% of the buttons on your screen. For Level 2, you are using exactly two tools: the Main Editor Window and the Copilot Chat Sidebar.

title: Level 2: AI in Your Editor
text:
Standardizing the workflow








Step
Before
After




Organize
In a browser, describe your project in a chat window with no file access
In the editor, open the chat sidebar and describe what you want to build; AI proposes structure and logic before a single file exists


Write
In a browser, copy code from chat, switch windows, paste manually
In the editor, ghost text appears inline as you type; press Tab to accept, no switching


Test
In a browser, copy the error message, open a new chat, re-explain the context
In the editor, click the Copilot icon next to the terminal error and AI reads the crash log directly


Share
In a browser, write your own commit message
In the editor, click the sparkle button in the Source Control panel and AI reads your diff and writes it


Deploy
Out of scope for this level
—



Level 2 has two keys: Tab accepts what AI proposes. Ctrl+I opens Copilot Chat. Everything else is reading and deciding.

title: Level 2: AI in Your Editor
text:
The exercises
Twelve exercises across four phases. Each one proves a specific capability that browser chat cannot match. Do them in order; the compound interest project builds across phases and the terminal does not appear until Exercise 9.




Exercise
AI capability




1
Blueprint prompt
Copilot Chat plans project structure from a plain-English description


2
Scaffold generation
Agent mode creates folders and files directly


3
Comment-driven generation
Ghost text completes a function from a comment spec


4
Finish starter code
Inline edit completes a function from its comments


5
Cross-file context
#filename references give the AI access to multiple files at once


6
Explain a block
Right-click → Explain generates an on-demand explanation


7
Spot a bug
Copilot Chat reviews a function for bugs and edge cases


8
Refactor for performance
Right-click → Review identifies a performance problem and the fix


9
Terminal crash fix
Terminal error hook connects a crash directly to AI


10
Generate a docstring
/doc generates documentation in two keystrokes


11
Generate pytest cases
Copilot Chat generates a full test suite from a description


12
Git commit via sparkle button
Sparkle button generates a commit message from the staged diff

title: Level 2: AI in Your Editor
text:
Organize & plan
Before writing a single line of code, use AI to think through structure. This is the habit that separates organized projects from tangled ones.


Exercise 1 — Blueprint prompt
Action: Open VS Code. Open the Copilot Chat sidebar. Do not open any files yet. Send this prompt:
I want to build a Python project that calculates compound interest
over time for a given principal, rate, and number of years. It
should also compare different compounding frequencies (annual,
monthly, daily). Before writing any code, describe the structure
and logic — what functions are needed, what they take as input,
and what they return.
Read the response. If anything is unclear, ask a follow-up before moving on.



Exercise 2 — Scaffold generation
Action: In the same chat session, send this prompt:
Now create the folder and file structure for this project. I need
you to actually create the folders and empty files — not just
suggest commands for me to run. Keep it simple: no frameworks,
just clean Python modules.
In agent mode, Copilot will create the folders and files directly. To switch: select “Agent” from the dropdown at the top of the chat panel before sending the prompt.
If you are in standard chat mode instead, Copilot will give you terminal commands. Run them in the VS Code integrated terminal (Ctrl+`).
Takeaway: AI generates and builds the project skeleton from a plain-English description. You reviewed the plan before committing to any structure — that is the right order of operations.


Why this works — and how to scale it
You just described a goal in plain English and got back a structure. That is not a trick; it is a pattern. AI works best as a consumer of structured context, not as a generator of structure from scratch in every new chat.
When you open a new chat and give a vague description, the AI is guessing at your intent. When you open a new chat and hand it a precise spec, the AI is executing against known constraints. Those are completely different quality levels — and the difference compounds over time.
In serious AI-assisted codebases, the folder structure reflects this:

spec/ — what the thing is: goals, constraints, non-goals
design/ — how it is structured: architecture, data model, component map
context/ — what AI needs to know each session: current state, decisions made, open questions
tasks/ — discrete, completable units of work

You are not there yet — this is Level 2. But the habit starts here: write down what you are building before you ask AI to build it. The documents are the project. The chat is just execution.
If you ever feel like you are constantly backpedaling with AI, or moving in the wrong direction instead of making progress, the cause is almost always the same: you started building before you finished specifying. The fix is not a better prompt — it is better documents.

title: Level 2: AI in Your Editor
text:
Writing code
Three exercises, each demonstrating a different way AI accelerates writing inside the editor. Keep the terminal panel closed for now; you will not need it until Exercise 9.


Exercise 3 — Comment-driven generation
Action: utils.py is the utility file from the project scaffold you created in Exercise 2 — where shared helper functions live that are used across the project.
Open your utils.py file. Type this comment and press Enter:
# Validate that principal, rate, and years are all positive numbers.
# Raise a ValueError with a descriptive message if any are not.
Wait. Ghost text should appear suggesting the full function body. Read it carefully before pressing Tab to accept.
Fallback — if nothing appears after a few seconds, send this in Copilot Chat:
Write a Python function that validates principal, rate, and years
are all positive numbers. Raise a ValueError with a descriptive
message for each invalid case. No external libraries.
Takeaway: A clear comment is a specification. The more precisely you describe the intent, the more accurately AI executes it. You described what you wanted; AI wrote the syntax. That is the trade at Level 2.



Exercise 4 — Finish starter code
Action: Create compound.py and paste this starter code:
def calculate_compound_interest(principal: float, rate: float,
                                years: int, n: int = 12) -> float:
    # Calculate compound interest using the standard formula.
    # principal: starting amount
    # rate: annual interest rate as a decimal (e.g. 0.05 for 5%)
    # years: number of years to compound
    # n: compounding frequency per year (default: monthly)
    # Return the final balance after the compounding period.
Place your cursor at the end of the last comment line. Press Ctrl+I to open Copilot Chat and send:
Complete this function based on the comments.
Review the generated body before accepting.
(You may also see ghost text appear automatically — if so, press Tab to accept it instead of using Ctrl+I.)
Note on the formula: The function should return the final balance — the full amount including the original principal — using the standard compound interest formula: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt). If AI returns only the interest earned (the growth above the principal), ask it to revise: "Return the final balance, not just the interest earned."
Takeaway: Describing intent in comments before writing code produces accurate completions. Ctrl+I gives you direct control; ghost text is the passive version of the same thing.



Exercise 5 — Cross-file context
Action: Create a second file called main.py. Open Copilot Chat and send:
I have a file called #compound.py that contains a function called
calculate_compound_interest. In #main.py, import that function and
call it with a principal of 1000, a rate of 0.05, and 10 years.
Print the result formatted to two decimal places.
Review what it generates. Check that the import path is correct and the function call matches the signature in compound.py.
Takeaway: The # symbol pulls a specific file into context mid-conversation. AI sees across files simultaneously. Browser chat would require copy-pasting both files before asking the same question.

title: Level 2: AI in Your Editor
text:
Understand, debug & refactor
Four exercises that use AI as a reviewer and optimizer. This is where in-editor AI earns its place.


Exercise 6 — Explain a block
Action: Select the entire calculate_compound_interest function in compound.py. Right-click and choose Copilot → Explain This.
Read the explanation. Then send this follow-up in chat:
What would happen if rate were passed as 5 instead of 0.05?
Would this function catch that mistake?
Takeaway: AI as an on-demand explainer for your own code. Works on any code you didn’t write too: inherited projects, library internals, examples from online.



Exercise 7 — Spot a bug
Action: Add this function to compound.py:
def compare_compounding(principal: float, rate: float, years: int) -> dict:
    results = {}
    for label, n in [("annual", 1), ("monthly", 12), ("daily", 365)]:
        results[label] = principal * (1 + rate / n) ** (n * years) - principal
    return results
Select the function. Open Copilot Chat and send:
Review this function for correctness. Are there any bugs — inputs
that would produce a wrong result or raise an error? Also identify
any edge cases I haven't handled. Be specific about where each
problem is and why it occurs.
If you see a Copilot debug icon appear next to the function, you can click it to trigger an automatic review — this is a newer Copilot feature that may or may not be available depending on your version. The manual prompt above always works.
Note on the bug: This function is intended to compare how much your money grows across different compounding frequencies: it should return the final balance for each frequency, just like calculate_compound_interest. Instead, it subtracts principal at the end, so it returns only the interest earned (the growth above what you started with). The formula should be principal * (1 + rate / n) ** (n * years) with no subtraction. Beyond the intent mismatch, a negative rate also produces a mathematically nonsensical result with no warning because there is no input validation. See what AI catches and what fix it proposes.
Takeaway: AI as a quality check. This kind of structural review would take careful reading and testing to catch manually. It took one prompt.



Exercise 8 — Refactor for performance
Action: Create pipeline.py and paste this function:
import pandas as pd

def process_sales(df: pd.DataFrame, year: int) -> pd.DataFrame:
    results = []
    for i in range(len(df)):
        row = df.iloc[i]
        if row["year"] == year:
            margin = (row["revenue"] - row["cost"]) / row["revenue"]
            results.append({
                "product": row["product"],
                "revenue": row["revenue"],
                "margin": margin
            })
    summary = pd.DataFrame(results)
    summary = summary.sort_values("margin")
    total = 0
    for i in range(len(summary)):
        total = total + summary.iloc[i]["revenue"]
    summary["revenue_share"] = summary["revenue"] / total
    return summary
Select the entire function. Right-click and choose Copilot → Review and Comment (or Refactor, depending on your version).
Read what AI identifies. You don’t need to apply the refactored version — the point is seeing AI explain why row-by-row iteration is slow and what the vectorized alternative looks like.
Takeaway: AI knows every vectorization idiom in pandas and can identify the performance problem with one right-click. You didn’t need to search documentation or guess at the issue.



Exercise 9 — Terminal crash fix
Action: This is the first exercise where the terminal appears. Open it in VS Code with Ctrl+`.
In main.py, deliberately introduce a type error by passing a string instead of a number:
result = calculate_compound_interest("1000", 0.05, 10)
Run the file. The terminal will show a TypeError. Look for the small Copilot icon that appears next to the error output in the terminal panel. Click it and select Explain using Copilot or Fix using Copilot.
Read the explanation before applying any fix.
Takeaway: The terminal error hook connects execution failures directly to AI without copy-pasting. AI reads the crash log, the stack trace, and your code simultaneously. This is the first preview of what Level 3 looks like: where AI lives in the terminal full-time.

title: Level 2: AI in Your Editor
text:
Test, document & share
Three exercises that close the loop — verification, documentation, and version control. Each uses a native VS Code UI element.


Exercise 10 — Generate a docstring
Action: Place your cursor inside calculate_compound_interest in compound.py, on the line just below the def line. Open Copilot Chat and type:
/doc
A Google-style docstring should be generated and inserted at the correct location. Review it — does it accurately describe the parameters and return value?
Takeaway: Professional documentation in under 5 seconds. Ctrl+I plus /doc is the full instruction. You described the intent with two keystrokes; AI read the function and wrote the documentation.



Exercise 11 — Generate pytest cases
Action: Open Copilot Chat and send:
Write pytest functions for calculate_compound_interest. Cover the
normal case, edge cases (rate of 0, years of 0), and invalid inputs
that should raise ValueError. Use only pytest, no fixtures.
Save the output to test_compound.py. Read each test before running it — AI-generated tests occasionally test the wrong behavior. Then run:
pytest test_compound.py -v
If tests fail with import or module errors, confirm your .venv is active and pytest is installed (pip install pytest).



Exercise 12 — Git commit via sparkle button
Action: Stage all your changes using the Source Control panel in VS Code (the branch icon in the left sidebar).
In the commit message input box at the top of the Source Control panel, look for the sparkle / Copilot icon. Click it. AI reads your staged diff and generates a commit message.
Review the message, edit if needed, then commit.
Takeaway: AI writes the commit message by reading what changed, not what you tell it changed. This is the native UI doing what browser chat cannot: reading your diff directly without you copying it out first.

title: Level 2: AI in Your Editor
text:
Prompt engineering
AI matches whatever scope you give it. These four principles separate productive sessions from overwhelming ones.

1. Give context before the ask
Describe what the project does and what role this code plays before making the request. Without context, AI fills gaps with assumptions.

Too thin: “Write a function that validates input.”
With context: “I’m building a compound interest calculator. Write a function that validates that principal, rate, and years are all positive numbers and raises a ValueError with a descriptive message for each invalid case.”



2. Constrain the scope explicitly
Without limits, AI defaults to comprehensive — more than you need, more than you can review. Explicit constraints keep the output manageable.

Too open: “Add error handling.”
Constrained: “Add a try/except block to the calculate_compound_interest call in main.py only. Catch ValueError and print the message. Don’t touch anything else.”



3. Ask for a plan before you ask for code
For anything larger than a single function, ask AI to describe its approach first. Exercise 1 does this explicitly — it is the right habit for any new project.

“Before writing any code, describe how you’d structure a function that compares compounding frequencies. What edge cases would you handle?”



4. Tell it your level and what you want to learn
“I’m new to pandas” produces simpler methods and more explanation. “Explain your reasoning alongside the code” gets you something to learn from, not just copy.

“I’m learning pandas. Write this using basic operations and add a comment on each line explaining what it does.”



Example prompt combining all four
// context
I'm building a compound interest calculator in Python. I have a
function called calculate_compound_interest that takes principal,
rate, years, and n (compounding frequency). I need a function that
compares the final balance across annual, monthly, and daily
compounding for the same inputs.

// constraints
Keep it under 15 lines. Use only the standard library.
Return a plain dictionary with frequency labels as keys.

// plan before code
Before writing the function, describe how the outputs will differ
across compounding frequencies and what edge cases exist.
Then write the code.

// level
I'm comfortable with Python basics but newer to financial math.
Add a comment explaining the compound interest formula.

title: Level 2: AI in Your Editor
text:
Editor & context

Your editor, your choice
Everything on this page uses VS Code with GitHub Copilot. If you are using PyCharm, Cursor, or another AI-capable editor, the exercises map directly — the features have different names but the same purpose. Ask your editor’s AI assistant to walk you through the equivalent of each exercise. Refactor, inline edit, explain, and test generation all exist in every major AI editor.


How file context works
When you open a file and open the Copilot Chat sidebar, the AI has already read your open file. For files you want to reference explicitly, open them in VS Code first — Copilot reads all open files as part of its context. You can also attach specific files using the file attachment button in the chat panel.
File context becomes meaningful starting at Exercise 5. The earlier exercises work from plain-English descriptions alone.

title: Level 2: AI in Your Editor
text:
What comes next: Level 3
Level 2 keeps AI inside the editor. It reads your files and proposes changes, but it cannot run anything. You still execute code in the terminal and carry results back into the chat manually.
Level 3 removes that constraint. A terminal agent like Claude Code has access to your full computing environment: it executes scripts, reads the output, installs packages, modifies files, and iterates until a task is complete. Exercise 9 above was the first preview of that boundary. Level 3 is what happens when you remove it entirely.

title: Python & AI — The Modern Workflow
text:
Learn the essential tools of modern Python development and how they work together in a real workflow.
→ Explore the tools · See how they connect

title: Python & AI — The Modern Workflow
text:
Learn the essential tools of modern Python development and how they work together in a real workflow.
→ Explore the tools · See how they connect

title: Python & AI — The Modern Workflow
text:
The core workflow
This is the basic sequence every Python project follows.



Step
Focus




Organize
Set up your environment and install packages


Write
Use your IDE and AI assistant to write code


Test
Run code and debug errors


Share
Track changes with Git and sync to GitHub


Deploy
Ship your project and iterate

title: Python & AI — The Modern Workflow
text:
What you’ll need
No matter how many tools you see, they all do one of five things:
01 — The Editor / IDE Where you write and read code. An IDE (Integrated Development Environment) is a text editor with superpowers: it understands your code, highlights errors, helps you navigate, and runs your programs. Examples: VS Code · PyCharm · Cursor
Learn about IDEs →
02 — The AI Assistant A model that helps you write, explain, debug, and refactor code. Can live inside your IDE or as a separate chat interface. Transforms what a solo developer can accomplish in a day. Examples: Claude · GitHub Copilot · ChatGPT · JetBrains AI
Learn about AI tools →
03 — Python Environment (venv) A self-contained Python installation for each project. Keeps your projects isolated so their dependencies don’t conflict. This is about managing packages and versions, not about AI. Every professional Python project has one. Examples: venv · conda · uv
Learn about environments →
04 — Version Control A system that tracks every change to your code, lets you go back in time, and enables multiple people to work on the same project without overwriting each other. Examples: Git · GitHub · GitLab · Bitbucket
Learn about Git & GitHub →
05 — Package Management How you install, update, and manage the libraries your code depends on. Works alongside your virtual environment to keep everything reproducible and shareable. Examples: pip · conda · uv
Learn about packages →

title: Python & AI — The Modern Workflow
text:
Three questions
Every developer, beginner or expert, is implicitly answering three questions. Understanding these questions means you can evaluate any new tool you encounter, not just the ones on this site.







Question
Answer




Where do I write and review code?
An editor or IDE: VS Code, PyCharm, Cursor, and others


Where do I run things?
A terminal: where code executes, scripts run, and agents live


How much can the AI see and do?
This is the levels question, and it’s what determines how powerful your workflow is

title: Python & AI — The Modern Workflow
text:
Five levels of AI integration
All of these levels use the same underlying AI model. What changes is how much of your environment the AI can see and act on, and how much you have to do manually in between. Knowing where you are shows you what to learn next. Read more here.
Level 0–1: Browser chat + copy-paste You’re using ChatGPT or Claude in a browser tab. You describe a problem, get code back, paste it into your editor, run it, hit an error, copy the error back up, and repeat. Or you have Copilot in your IDE doing inline suggestions, helpful but still basically a chat loop. The core limitation: the AI has no access to your project. It can’t see your files, your errors in context, or how your code is structured. Every exchange starts from scratch. The Get Started page walks you through a complete setup in VS Code and PyCharm, including AI from day one.
Level 2: AI in your editor The AI can now read and edit your actual project files. You describe what you want; it proposes changes across multiple files at once. You review the diffs and decide what to keep. The AI isn’t just suggesting; it’s doing. The shift here isn’t about a specific tool, it’s a pattern: the AI has access to your codebase, and you’re in the role of reviewer rather than typist. This works in VS Code with GitHub Copilot agent mode, and in other editors that support in-context AI. The workflow is the same regardless of which tool you use. → Level 2: AI in Your Editor
Level 3+: Dedicated coding agents The AI moves out of the editor and into your terminal, where it has access to your full computing environment. This means it can run code and see the output, not just read files. It can execute scripts, install packages, use system tools, read and write anywhere on your filesystem, and iterate on its own until a task is complete. You describe an outcome; it figures out the steps. The IDE is still useful for reviewing what the agent produced, but it’s no longer required to run the task. Claude Code is the primary example covered here; OpenAI Codex CLI and Gemini CLI follow the same pattern. → Level 3+
Level 4: Connected agents You extend the agent with live connections to the outside world via MCP (Model Context Protocol), a standard that lets the agent pull in information from beyond your computer: current library documentation, web search, databases, and other services. Without this, an agent is limited to what it already knows, which can lag months behind. With it, the agent can look up the exact API it needs right now, query your database while it codes, or search the web mid-task. Superpowers, Context7, and Sequential Thinking from the Level 3+ page are Level 4 tools layered on top of a Level 3 setup.
Level 5: Fully autonomous runs You give the agent a goal, set it running in a container, and come back an hour later. Real, but requires significant setup, careful scoping, and thorough review of the result.
All levels use the same underlying AI model. The difference is how much structure, access, and autonomy you give it.


“The tools change. The workflow doesn’t.”

No matter which editor, AI assistant, or package manager you use, every project follows the same underlying sequence of setup, writing, testing, and sharing.
Learning the pattern means you can switch tools at any time without starting from scratch.
→ See the full workflow

title: Python & AI — The Modern Workflow
section: Where do you want to start?
text:
Where do you want to start?

The Tools: Deep dives into every major tool category, including IDEs, AI assistants, environments, Git, and packages.
How It Connects: See how the tools map to each step of the workflow and what a real project structure looks like.
Get Started: Step-by-step setup guides and AI workflows for VS Code and PyCharm. Your path into real AI-assisted coding.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
At Level 3, the AI runs in your terminal. You describe a goal; it reads your files, writes code, runs scripts, and iterates on failures until the task is done. You review the result. What makes this more capable than an editor assistant is not what the agent can see; it is that it can execute. In the editor, you run the tests and bring errors back to the AI. Here, the agent runs that loop itself.
A terminal agent is the right tool when the task requires execution: running tests, iterating across files, working through a multi-step process. For inline suggestions while you’re actively writing code, the IDE is still the natural fit. The two complement each other; most people use both.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
The new development cycle








Step
In the editor
In the terminal




Organize
Describe the goal in chat; AI proposes structure
Describe the goal; AI reads your existing files and proposes or creates structure


Write
AI suggests edits inline; you accept or reject
AI writes to files directly; you review what changed


Test
You run tests and bring failures back to AI
AI runs tests, reads the output, and iterates on failures itself


Share
AI writes a commit message from your diff; you commit
AI can stage, write the message, and commit; you review before or after


Deploy
Not covered
Scripted pipelines can be handed to the agent; it runs them and reports the result

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
The interaction loop
Every terminal AI agent works the same way: you describe a goal, the agent reads your files and environment, acts, reads the output, and reports back. You review and continue or redirect.
Understanding this loop changes how you write prompts. You are not giving instructions to someone who will ask for clarification — you are describing a goal for an agent that acts immediately. Keep goals narrow enough to review comfortably.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
Your project

Context engineering is the new prompt engineering.

The agent only has access to what you point it at. Your folder structure is not just organization — it defines what the agent can see, and specific files carry your standing instructions between sessions. A well-structured project makes the agent faster and more reliable from the first prompt.
my-project/
├── context.md         ← standing instructions, read every session
├── progress.md        ← handoff notes between sessions
├── docs/
│   └── plans/         ← implementation plans
└── src/

The context file
Every session starts cold. The agent has no memory of previous sessions — no knowledge of your project structure, conventions, or the decisions you made last week. A context file, a markdown file in your project root, is how you fix that. Write your standing instructions there once; the agent reads it at the start of every session. Keep it factual: project structure, conventions, constraints that apply to every task. Specifics for the current task go in the prompt.
The folders you list and the directories you grant the agent access to form the same boundary. Start narrow: read and write within the project directory, run test and build commands you already use, no broader filesystem access. An agent with write access to your filesystem and permission to run arbitrary commands can make changes you didn’t intend. Review what it’s done at each step, especially early on.


The context window
The context window is the total amount of text — instructions, files, code, and conversation history — that the model can hold at once. As a session runs, everything accumulates there: your instructions, the files the agent read, the code it wrote, the test output, your feedback. This window has a fixed size, passed back and forth with every message. Once it fills, the agent starts dropping the oldest entries to make room.
In practice, a session that starts well can become unreliable as context fills. The agent forgets constraints you set earlier, duplicates code it already wrote, or loses track of decisions. This is not a defect in any specific tool — it is a fundamental property of how these models work.
Most tools offer a compaction command to summarize the current context and continue without restarting. This sounds useful but usually isn’t. Compaction compresses the window, but degraded reasoning from before the compaction still affects the session. Starting fresh with a written handoff produces better results.

Friends don’t let friends compact.



Between sessions
End sessions before context degrades. Most tools display context usage somewhere, and if yours doesn’t, it is worth making it visible. When context approaches 50%, end the session:
Write a summary of what we've done and what's left to progress.md
The next session reads that file and continues cleanly. Treat sessions as bounded units of work — end when the immediate task is done, not when the window is full. Avoid open-ended prompts like “clean up the whole codebase.” Break large goals into specific, bounded tasks.


Planning
Ad-hoc prompts produce worse results than structured ones. A structured workflow replaces guessing with agreement: describe the goal, clarify requirements, produce a plan in docs/plans/, then execute against it. Each phase is short. The execution is faster and produces fewer unintended changes.
Scope explicitly when executing. Vague file access reliably produces unintended changes — name the files and constraints before the agent starts writing.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
MCP
Model Context Protocol is an open standard for connecting AI agents to external tools. Instead of pasting documentation into a prompt or building a one-off integration script, you configure an MCP server once and the agent can call it in any session.
An MCP server is a small program the agent invokes natively, indistinguishable from a built-in capability once configured. Common uses: pulling current library documentation, querying a database, running a custom analysis, accessing an API. Configure it once; it is available in every session.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
section: Try it out with Claude Code
text:
Try it out with Claude Code
The concepts above apply to any terminal AI tool. Claude Code is one of them. If you want to try it, this section covers an efficient setup: the tool itself plus seven integrations that implement what we’ve discussed. A project sandbox and quick reference table follow below.
Install Claude Code and find platform-specific setup instructions at the official documentation:
Install Claude Code
Navigate to your project and type claude to start a session. Many developers keep their IDE open to review files while running Claude Code in a terminal alongside it. Claude makes the changes; the IDE shows you what changed.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
Sandbox
Create a dedicated folder anywhere on your computer and drop in this structure. It gives you a clean place to experiment without touching any existing project — open it in your terminal and you are ready to go.
my-project/
├── CLAUDE.md                  ← project context, read every session
├── progress.md                ← session handoff notes
├── docs/
│   └── plans/                 ← implementation plans go here
├── .claude/
│   └── skills/                ← custom workflows, invoked with /skill-name
├── src/
└── tests/
Full documentation for CLAUDE.md and skills is in the official docs.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
Quick reference



#
Integration
What it adds
Where to get it




01
Statusline
Context %, session cost, model, git branch
Claude Code docs


02
Superpowers
Sub-agent workflows: brainstorm → plan → execute
/plugin inside Claude Code


03
Sequential Thinking
Chain-of-thought reasoning before complex tasks
Ask Claude: “install the sequential thinking MCP server”


04
Context7
Current library docs at prompt time
/plugin inside Claude Code


05
Warp
Split panes, block output
warp.dev


06
Happy Engineering
Full terminal access from your phone
happy.engineering


07
Skills
Reusable workflows for repetitive tasks
Create .claude/skills/skillname.md

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
01 — Statusline
Make context usage visible: the most critical metric in a session
The statusline adds a persistent bar at the bottom of your terminal showing context window percentage, session cost, model name, elapsed time, and git status. Without it, you notice context degradation when Claude starts making mistakes. With it, you can end sessions before quality drops.
Find install instructions in the Claude Code documentation or by asking Claude: How do I install the statusline?

Windows note: The setup wizard sometimes fails on Windows. If the guided setup does not complete, install manually: npx cc-statusline@latest in your terminal.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
02 — Superpowers
Structured sub-agent workflows: the highest-impact single install
Superpowers is a Claude Code plugin that implements the planning and sub-agent workflow described above. Instead of one session accumulating context until it degrades, your main Claude instance dispatches focused sub-agents — each with its own fresh context window. The orchestrator receives summaries, not full workloads.
Superpowers enforces the brainstorm → plan → execute workflow: clarify requirements before writing anything, produce a spec, execute against it.
Install via the Claude Code plugin menu — type /plugin inside a session and find Superpowers in the list.

If you install one thing from this page, install Superpowers.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
03 — Sequential Thinking
Structured reasoning before complex tasks
Sequential Thinking is an MCP server that adds explicit chain-of-thought reasoning to Claude Code. Before acting on complex tasks, Claude works through a visible step-by-step reasoning process — understand the problem, identify constraints, consider approaches, then implement.
Ask Claude to install it: Please install the sequential thinking MCP server

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
04 — Context7
Current library documentation: eliminates hallucinated APIs
Claude Code’s training data has a cutoff date. When you ask it to use a library, it works from training data that may be months out of date — confidently using deprecated methods or renamed APIs. Context7 pulls current, version-specific documentation from live repositories and injects it into Claude’s context at prompt time.
Install via the Claude Code plugin menu — type /plugin inside a session and find Context7 in the list.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
05 — Warp
A better terminal for running Claude Code
Warp is a modern terminal — a replacement for Windows Terminal, Mac Terminal, or the VS Code integrated terminal. You run Claude Code inside Warp.
Split panes let you run Claude on one side and browse your project files on the other. When Claude writes a plan to a file, you can read it while Claude is still working. Each command and its output is a discrete block; no scrolling through walls of output to find what Claude ran.
Download from Warp — available for Mac, Linux, and Windows. Free to use.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
06 — Happy Engineering
Full terminal access from your phone
Happy Engineering runs a live terminal session on your computer that you control from your phone’s browser. Setup takes about a minute — no accounts, no configuration, simpler than any remote-access alternative. When you connect from your phone, you are controlling the actual terminal on your machine — every integration you have configured is available. Tasks you start on your phone continue running on your computer.
Install from Happy Engineering. Free and open source.

title: Level 3: AI in Your Terminal
text:
07 — Skills
Reusable workflows for tasks you do repeatedly
Skills are markdown files in .claude/skills/ that Claude Code reads when you invoke them. Instead of re-explaining a workflow every session, you encode it once and call it by name.
.claude/
  skills/
    add-module.md    ← invoked with /add-module
    run-analysis.md  ← invoked with /run-analysis
Describe the repetitive task in detail and ask Claude to build the skill for you. After a few iterations, it becomes a reliable automation available in every session.

title: The Tools
text:
Where you write and understand code.
Simply put
An IDE is like a word processor, but for code. Notepad can write code. An IDE understands code. It highlights mistakes as you type, lets you jump to any function definition instantly, runs your programs with one click, and shows you the output inline. It’s the central place where everything else (AI, Git, environments) plugs in.
Going deeper
IDEs provide language servers (LSP) that analyze your code in real time, offering type checking, autocomplete, and inline documentation. They integrate debuggers (breakpoints, step-through execution, variable inspection), test runners, linters, and formatters. Modern editors are highly extensible via plugins, which is why the same editor can support Python, JavaScript, Rust, or SQL depending on what you install.
VS Code — Microsoft · Free · All platforms
A lightweight but powerful editor that has become the default choice for most developers. It starts fast, has an enormous extension marketplace, and supports every language. Python support comes via Microsoft’s Python extension.

Free and open source
Massive extension library
Excellent AI integration (Copilot, Claude, etc.)
Fast startup, lightweight
Python features require extensions
Less opinionated — you configure your own workflow

Best for: Most people. Especially if you work across multiple languages or want maximum flexibility.

PyCharm — JetBrains · Free (Community) or Paid (Pro) · All platforms
Built exclusively for Python (and web development in the Pro version). PyCharm understands Python deeply out of the box, no extensions needed. It handles virtual environments, testing, databases, and debugging with more depth than VS Code’s Python extension.

Deepest Python support available
Virtual environment management built-in
Excellent debugger and test runner
JetBrains AI built-in (Pro)
Heavier, slower to start than VS Code
Pro version costs money (~$100/yr, free for students)

Best for: Developers working primarily in Python who want everything configured for them.

Cursor — Anysphere · Free tier + Paid · All platforms
A fork of VS Code built from the ground up around AI. Every feature is designed to maximize how much AI can help: it can see your whole codebase, understand context across files, and generate entire features at once. Feels like VS Code but with a much deeper AI layer.

Most advanced AI integration available
Familiar if you know VS Code
Supports Claude, GPT-4, and others as the underlying model
Paid tier required for heavy AI use
Newer — less mature than VS Code or PyCharm

Best for: Developers who want AI deeply embedded in every part of their editor experience.

Jupyter Notebook / JupyterLab — Project Jupyter · Free · Browser-based
Not a traditional IDE; Jupyter is a notebook environment where code, output, and explanatory text live together in one document. Run a cell, see the result immediately below it. Essential in data science and research. Can also be used inside VS Code and PyCharm as an extension.

Immediate output: ideal for exploration
Mixes code, charts, and text in one document
Industry standard in data science
Not suited for large, structured applications
Version control is messier with notebooks

Best for: Exploratory analysis, teaching, and research where seeing output immediately matters.

title: The Tools
text:
Where you write and understand code.
Simply put
An IDE is like a word processor, but for code. Notepad can write code. An IDE understands code. It highlights mistakes as you type, lets you jump to any function definition instantly, runs your programs with one click, and shows you the output inline. It’s the central place where everything else (AI, Git, environments) plugs in.
Going deeper
IDEs provide language servers (LSP) that analyze your code in real time, offering type checking, autocomplete, and inline documentation. They integrate debuggers (breakpoints, step-through execution, variable inspection), test runners, linters, and formatters. Modern editors are highly extensible via plugins, which is why the same editor can support Python, JavaScript, Rust, or SQL depending on what you install.
VS Code — Microsoft · Free · All platforms
A lightweight but powerful editor that has become the default choice for most developers. It starts fast, has an enormous extension marketplace, and supports every language. Python support comes via Microsoft’s Python extension.

Free and open source
Massive extension library
Excellent AI integration (Copilot, Claude, etc.)
Fast startup, lightweight
Python features require extensions
Less opinionated — you configure your own workflow

Best for: Most people. Especially if you work across multiple languages or want maximum flexibility.

PyCharm — JetBrains · Free (Community) or Paid (Pro) · All platforms
Built exclusively for Python (and web development in the Pro version). PyCharm understands Python deeply out of the box, no extensions needed. It handles virtual environments, testing, databases, and debugging with more depth than VS Code’s Python extension.

Deepest Python support available
Virtual environment management built-in
Excellent debugger and test runner
JetBrains AI built-in (Pro)
Heavier, slower to start than VS Code
Pro version costs money (~$100/yr, free for students)

Best for: Developers working primarily in Python who want everything configured for them.

Cursor — Anysphere · Free tier + Paid · All platforms
A fork of VS Code built from the ground up around AI. Every feature is designed to maximize how much AI can help: it can see your whole codebase, understand context across files, and generate entire features at once. Feels like VS Code but with a much deeper AI layer.

Most advanced AI integration available
Familiar if you know VS Code
Supports Claude, GPT-4, and others as the underlying model
Paid tier required for heavy AI use
Newer — less mature than VS Code or PyCharm

Best for: Developers who want AI deeply embedded in every part of their editor experience.

Jupyter Notebook / JupyterLab — Project Jupyter · Free · Browser-based
Not a traditional IDE; Jupyter is a notebook environment where code, output, and explanatory text live together in one document. Run a cell, see the result immediately below it. Essential in data science and research. Can also be used inside VS Code and PyCharm as an extension.

Immediate output: ideal for exploration
Mixes code, charts, and text in one document
Industry standard in data science
Not suited for large, structured applications
Version control is messier with notebooks

Best for: Exploratory analysis, teaching, and research where seeing output immediately matters.

title: The Tools
text:
AI Assistants
A collaborator that never leaves your editor.
Simply put
An AI assistant is a tool that can read your code and talk to you about it: writing new code, explaining what something does, finding bugs, suggesting improvements, or generating tests. Some live inside your IDE as extensions. Some are separate chat interfaces. Some are both. The best ones feel like having a very knowledgeable pair programmer on call at all times.
Going deeper
Modern coding AI assistants are built on large language models (LLMs) fine-tuned for code. The key distinction is context window: how much of your code the model can see at once. A larger context window means smarter, more coherent suggestions. IDE-integrated tools can stream completions inline (autocomplete-style), while chat-based tools let you have a full conversation about architecture, debugging, or design choices. The distinction between “inline assistant” and “chat assistant” is blurring; most tools now do both.

title: The Tools
text:
Virtual Environments
Keeping your projects clean and self-contained.
Simply put
Imagine you have two Python projects. Project A needs version 1 of a library. Project B needs version 2 of the same library. If you install both on the same Python installation, they conflict. A virtual environment solves this — it’s a private, isolated copy of Python and its packages for each project. When you work on Project A, you activate its environment. Everything is separate.
Going deeper
A virtual environment is a directory containing a Python interpreter (or a symlink to one), a site-packages folder for installed libraries, and activation scripts. When activated, your shell’s PATH is modified so that python and pip refer to the environment’s copies, not the system installation. This makes your project reproducible: a requirements.txt or pyproject.toml captures exactly which packages and versions are needed, so anyone can recreate the environment from scratch.
The analogy
Think of a virtual environment like a shipping container. Everything your project needs is packed inside it: the right version of Python, the right libraries, nothing more. You can move the container to any machine, open it, and everything works. Without it, you’re throwing your tools into a shared toolbox with everyone else’s, and eventually something breaks.
venv — Built into Python · Free · All platforms
The built-in Python virtual environment tool. No installation required. Creates a .venv folder inside your project with everything isolated. The standard starting point for Python projects.
# Create a virtual environment
python -m venv .venv

# Activate it (Mac/Linux)
source .venv/bin/activate

# Activate it (Windows)
.venv\Scripts\activate
Best for: Most Python projects. Simple, reliable, no extra installation.

conda / Anaconda — Anaconda Inc · Free (conda) · All platforms
A package and environment manager that goes beyond Python — it can also manage non-Python dependencies (like C libraries used by NumPy or OpenCV). Anaconda comes bundled with hundreds of data science packages pre-installed. conda is the command-line tool alone.

Handles non-Python dependencies
Pre-packaged data science tools (Anaconda)
Heavier than venv
Can conflict with pip in subtle ways

Best for: Data science work, machine learning, or projects with complex C-extension dependencies.

uv — Astral · Free · All platforms
A new, extremely fast Python package installer and environment manager written in Rust. Replaces both pip and venv in one tool and is 10–100× faster. Rapidly gaining adoption in professional settings.

Dramatically faster than pip
Manages Python versions too
Drop-in replacement for pip and venv
Newer — less documentation and community resources

Best for: Developers who want the fastest possible toolchain. Growing toward being the new default.

title: The Tools
text:
Git & GitHub
How code is saved, shared, and collaborated on.
Simply put
Git is a tool that lives on your computer and tracks every change you make to your code, like a very detailed undo history that never expires. You can go back to any point in time, work on experiments without breaking anything, and merge changes from collaborators.
GitHub is a website where your Git history lives in the cloud, so your code is backed up, shareable, and others can contribute. They’re related but separate: Git is the technology; GitHub is the service built on top of it.
Going deeper
Git is a distributed version control system. Each repository contains a complete history of every commit, a snapshot of the codebase at a point in time. Branches allow parallel lines of development; merges (and rebases) combine them. GitHub adds: remote repository hosting, pull requests (code review workflow), Issues (bug/feature tracking), Actions (CI/CD automation), and a social layer. GitLab and Bitbucket offer similar services with different trade-offs.
Repository (repo): A folder that Git is tracking. Contains your code and its entire history.
Commit: A saved snapshot of your code at a moment in time. Like hitting “save,” but you also write a note about what changed and why.
Branch: A parallel version of your code. Make changes without affecting the main version. Merge when ready.
Checkout: Switches to another branch, another version of your project. git checkout -b creates a new branch and moves you onto it.
Push / Pull: Push sends your commits to GitHub. Pull brings GitHub’s latest changes to your machine.
Pull Request (PR): A request to merge your branch into the main branch. This is where code review happens on GitHub.
Clone: Download a repository from GitHub to your computer, gets the full history, not just the latest files.

Git vs GitHub vs GitLab vs Bitbucket
Git is the tool. GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket are services that host your Git repositories online. GitHub is by far the most popular, especially for open source. GitLab is popular in enterprises and has more built-in CI/CD features. Bitbucket is common in organizations already using Atlassian tools (Jira, Confluence). The Git commands you type are identical regardless of which service you use.

title: The Tools
text:
Package Management
Installing and managing the libraries your code needs.
Simply put
Python has a vast ecosystem of open-source libraries — tools for data analysis (pandas), making web requests (requests), machine learning (scikit-learn), and thousands more. Package managers are how you install these libraries into your project. Think of them like an app store for Python code — you ask for what you need, and the package manager downloads and installs it.
Going deeper
Python packages are distributed via PyPI (Python Package Index). A package manager resolves your package list, finds compatible versions of all dependencies (including dependencies-of-dependencies), downloads them, and places them in your virtual environment’s site-packages. A lock file records the exact versions installed, so that running the same install command on another machine six months later produces the exact same environment.
pip — Built into Python · Free
The standard Python package installer. Ships with Python. Used to install packages from PyPI with a single command. Works with a requirements.txt file to capture and share your project’s dependencies.
pip install pandas
pip install -r requirements.txt
pip freeze > requirements.txt
Best for: Most situations. If you’re not sure what to use, start here.

uv (as a package manager) — Astral · Free
uv can replace pip entirely — it’s a drop-in replacement that’s dramatically faster. The command interface is similar to pip, making the switch easy.
uv add pandas
uv sync
uv pip install -r requirements.txt
Best for: Developers who want the fastest possible install times.

requirements.txt and pyproject.toml
These are the two standard ways to declare what packages your project needs. requirements.txt is older and simpler — a plain list of packages. pyproject.toml is the modern standard — it also contains project metadata, tool configuration, and works with Poetry and uv. Both serve the same purpose: if someone else clones your project, they can install exactly what you’re using.

← Overview | How It Connects →
